1.pericope_label: The Great King over All the Earth
1.synthesis: Psalm 47 is a joyful enthronement hymn celebrating YHWH as king over all the earth, often associated with an annual festival of divine enthronement and connected in Christian reading to the Ascension. The opening summons is universal: “Clap your hands, all peoples! Shout to God with loud songs of joy! For the Lord, the Most High, is to be feared, a great king over all the earth” (Ps 47:1–2). His sovereignty over nations is declared: he subdued peoples under Israel, chose the heritage for them, the glory of Jacob whom he loved (47:3–4). Then the dramatic image: “God has gone up with a shout, the Lord with the sound of a trumpet” (47:5). The congregation is called to sing praises four times in two verses: “Sing praises to God, sing praises! Sing praises to our King, sing praises! For God is the King of all the earth; sing praises with a psalm!” (47:6–7). The closing affirmation: “God reigns over the nations; God sits on his holy throne. The princes of the peoples gather as the people of the God of Abraham. For the shields of the earth belong to God; he is highly exalted!” (47:8–9).
1.historical_context: Psalm 47 belongs to the group of enthronement psalms (Pss 47, 93, 96–99) that celebrate YHWH’s kingship with the proclamation “the Lord reigns.” These psalms have been associated with an annual New Year festival of divine enthronement (proposed by Sigmund Mowinckel), though the evidence for such a festival is debated. The image of God “going up” with a shout and the sound of a trumpet (47:5) parallels the procession of the ark in 2 Sam 6:15 and suggests a liturgical context of divine-presence procession. The gathering of “princes of the peoples...as the people of the God of Abraham” (47:9) envisions the eschatological ingathering of the nations into YHWH’s worship—an Abraham-shaped universalism: all nations shall be blessed in Abraham’s seed, and here the nations’ leaders join his people. The fourfold “sing praises” is unusual liturgical accumulation, each repetition adding urgency to the call.
1.literary_structure: Psalm 47 divides into two parallel strophes of approximately equal length, each ending with a royal affirmation. The first strophe (vv. 1–5) moves from universal summons to specific historical action (subduing peoples, choosing heritage) to the dramatic enthronement image (“God has gone up”). The second strophe (vv. 6–9) repeats the summons to praise in concentrated form and closes with the universal gathering of nations as YHWH’s own. The two strophes mirror each other: the first grounds YHWH’s kingship in his particular covenant with Israel; the second expands that kingship to encompass all nations and their rulers. The movement from particular to universal is the psalm’s theological argument: the God of Abraham is the king of all the earth.
1.theological_application: The universalism of Psalm 47 is not the erasure of Israel’s particularity but its eschatological expansion: the “princes of the peoples” gather not as conquerors of Israel but as those who have been brought near to the God of Abraham (47:9). Communities shaped by this psalm are given a vision of YHWH’s kingship that extends beyond all ethnic and political boundaries—“the shields of the earth belong to God” (47:9)—while remaining anchored in the particular covenant with Israel. The four-times “sing praises” models a worship that cannot be satisfied with a single repetition: the greatness of the king demands accumulation, not economy, of praise. The shout and trumpet of 47:5 are not merely historical memory but anticipatory: what happened in Israel’s cult is a foretaste of the cosmic enthronement that all creation will witness.
1.christology: “God has gone up with a shout, the Lord with the sound of a trumpet” (47:5) is the Ascension psalm: Christ ascending to the right hand of the Father, entering the heavenly sanctuary in triumph as the one who has been “mighty in battle” over sin and death. Paul applies the language of ascent to Christ in Ephesians 4:8–10, citing a related passage from Psalm 68:18: “he ascended on high, he led a host of captives.” The gathering of the nations’ princes as the people of the God of Abraham (47:9) is fulfilled in the church’s Gentile mission: “know then that it is those of faith who are the sons of Abraham” (Gal 3:7). The shout and trumpet will sound again at Christ’s return: “the Lord himself will descend from heaven with a cry of command, with the voice of an archangel, and with the sound of the trumpet of God” (1 Thess 4:16).
